agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature where three stakeholders (product, finance, operations) have provided conflicting requirements about when and whether an order can be cancelled.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, subject to a lifecycle of placement, invoicing, shipping, and potential cancellation."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled or shipped."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document generated for an order that locks in pricing and creates a billing obligation. Described by finance as irreversible."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer; the point after which even the PM agrees cancellation is no longer possible."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment a customer confirms an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this point."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can only be cancelled while it is in a cancellable state — before it ships.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Shipping involves physical dispatch of goods; once shipped, cancellation is a fundamentally different process (return/refund), not a cancellation.","testable_assertion":"Given an order with status SHIPPED, when a cancellation is attempted, then the system must reject it."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial commitment. If this rule holds, invoiced orders are permanently non-cancellable.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, when a cancellation is attempted, then the system must reject it (per finance's rule)."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. It could be changed (e.g., invoice at shipment instead) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, then an invoice is generated immediately upon placement."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order that has been shipped","when":"A cancellation is requested","then":"The system rejects the cancellation with an appropriate error message — all three stakeholders agree on this."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order that has just been placed (pre-shipment)","when":"A cancellation is requested","then":"UNRESOLVABLE — PM says allow it; finance + operations rules combined say reject it. Requires stakeholder resolution."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order exists","when":"The order is placed","then":"An invoice is generated at placement time to lock in pricing (per operations). This is the uncontested operations requirement."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation (seconds later)","expected_behavior":"Under PM's rule: allowed. Under finance+ops rules: denied because the order was invoiced at placement. This is the core contradiction — there is zero cancellation window."},{"scenario":"Order is placed but invoice generation fails or is delayed","expected_behavior":"If invoicing is asynchronous and briefly delayed, a tiny cancellation window might exist. But this is an implementation accident, not an intentional policy. Must not rely on race conditions for business logic."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether cancellation applies to unshipped items. Not addressed by any stakeholder."},{"scenario":"Credit memo or reversal instead of cancellation","expected_behavior":"Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but they may support credit memos/reversals as a distinct mechanism. This could be the resolution path but was never stated."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If cancellation is blocked entirely (finance wins), customers cannot cancel orders at all, leading to increased returns, chargebacks, and customer service costs. If cancellation is allowed (PM wins), finance must handle invoice reversals, impacting revenue recognition and accounting processes.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement for pricing lock-in. Allowing cancellation post-invoice forces a reversal workflow that operations has not described. Blocking cancellation forces a returns/refund workflow instead.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment. A system that invoices instantly and then blocks cancellation provides a terrible customer experience — effectively a no-cancellation policy with no warning.","risk":"HIGH. Implementing either interpretation without resolving the contradiction will violate one stakeholder's explicit requirement. Getting this wrong has legal implications (consumer protection laws in many jurisdictions require a cancellation window).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — determines whether they can cancel orders. A no-cancellation policy is hostile to customers and may violate consumer protection regulations."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Must handle either invoice reversals (if cancellation is allowed) or increased chargebacks/returns (if it is not)."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is decoupled from placement to enable cancellation, they lose their pricing lock-in mechanism."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is blocked, or must process manual reversals if the system lacks a proper cancellation-with-reversal flow."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Many jurisdictions mandate cooling-off periods for online orders. A system that makes cancellation impossible may be non-compliant."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements form a logical impossibility: PM allows pre-ship cancellation, finance blocks post-invoice cancellation, and operations invoices at placement. Combined, these mean orders are cancellable before shipping BUT also never cancellable because they are invoiced the instant they are placed. The spec is internally contradictory and cannot be implemented as stated. Confidence is very low because no correct implementation is possible without stakeholder resolution.","high_confidence_areas":["Post-shipment cancellation is universally blocked — all parties agree","The contradiction is real, not a misreading — the three rules are logically incompatible","Invoicing at placement is a policy choice, not a hard constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether finance would accept credit memos or invoice reversals as distinct from cancellation","Whether operations could decouple pricing lock-in from invoicing","Whether a cooling-off period is legally required in the applicable jurisdiction","Partial shipment cancellation policy"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements are accurately representing current policy","Invoicing is synchronous and occurs successfully at placement time","There is no existing invoice reversal or credit memo mechanism in play"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements create a logical impossibility. PM says orders are cancellable before shipment. Finance says invoiced orders are never cancellable. Operations says orders are invoiced at placement. Therefore: orders are cancellable before shipment AND orders are never cancellable (since invoicing happens at placement, before shipment). The cancellation window is empty.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Finance wins — no cancellation allowed post-invoice. PM must withdraw the cancellation feature. Customers use returns/refunds instead.","B: PM wins — allow cancellation pre-shipment, requiring finance to support invoice reversal or credit memo as part of cancellation. Invoicing is no longer truly irreversible.","C: Operations changes — decouple pricing lock-in from invoicing (e.g., use a price reservation mechanism). Invoice only at shipment. Cancellation window exists naturally.","D: Hybrid — introduce a time-limited cancellation window (e.g., 30 minutes) during which the invoice is held in DRAFT/PENDING status, not yet finalized. After the window, invoice finalizes and cancellation is blocked."],"recommendation":"Option D (hybrid) or Option C (decouple). Option D preserves all three stakeholders' intent: customers get a cancellation window (PM satisfied), invoicing remains irreversible once finalized (finance satisfied), and pricing is locked at placement via a draft invoice or price reservation (operations satisfied). Stakeholders must convene to agree on the window duration and draft-invoice semantics."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The requirements as stated cannot be implemented — they form a logical contradiction with zero cancellation window.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellable before shipment) is mutually exclusive with BR-2 + BR-3 combined (invoiced at placement, invoiced orders not cancellable)."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature where three stakeholders (product, finance, operations) have provided conflicting requirements about when and whether an order can be cancelled.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, subject to a lifecycle of placement, invoicing, shipping, and potential cancellation."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voi

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these requirements carefully.

Three statements:
1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction is clear:
- If orders are invoiced at placement (ops), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled (since they're invoiced immediately).
- But PM says orders can be cancelled before shipping.

This is a direct contradiction between explicit stakeholder requirements. 

So I'm flagging this as a genuine contradiction that needs resolution before I can proceed with the design.

This could be the resolution path but was never stated."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If cancellation is blocked entirely (finance wins), customers cannot cancel orders at all, leading to increased returns, chargebacks, and customer service costs. A system that makes cancellation impossible may be non-compliant."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Three explicit stakeholder requirements form a logical impossibility: PM allows pre-ship cancellation, finance blocks post-invoice cancellation, and operations invoices at placement. The spec is internally contradictory and cannot be implemented as stated. Confidence is very low because no correct implementation is possible without stakeholder resolution.","high_confidence_areas":["Post-shipment cancellation is universally blocked — all parties agree","The contradiction is real, not a misreading — the three rules are logically incompatible","Invoicing at placement is a policy choice, not a hard constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether finance would accept credit memos or invoice reversals as distinct from cancellation","Whether operations could decouple pricing lock-in from invoicing","Whether a cooling-off period is legally required in the applicable jurisdiction","Partial shipment cancellation policy"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements are accurately representing current policy","Invoicing is synchronous and occurs successfully at placement time","There is no existing invoice reversal or credit memo mechanism in play"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements create a logical impossibility. PM says orders are cancellable before shipment. Finance says invoiced orders are never cancellable. Operations says orders are invoiced at placement. Therefore: orders are cancellable before shipment AND orders are never cancellable (since invoicing happens at placement, before shipment). The cancellation window is empty.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance","Operations","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["A: Finance wins — no cancellation allowed post-invoice. PM must withdraw the cancellation feature. Customers use returns/refunds instead.","B: PM wins — allow cancellation pre-shipment, requiring finance to support invoice reversal or credit memo as part of cancellation. Invoicing is no longer truly irreversible.","C: Operations changes — decouple pricing lock-in from invoicing (e.g., use a price reservation mechanism). Invoice only at shipment. Cancellation window exists naturally.","D: Hybrid — introduce a time-limited cancellation window (e.g., 30 minutes) during which the invoice is held in DRAFT/PENDING status, not yet finalized. After the window, invoice finalizes and cancellation is blocked."],"recommendation":"Option D (hybrid) or Option C (decouple). Option D preserves all three stakeholders' intent: customers get a cancellation window (PM satisfied), invoicing remains irreversible once finalized (finance satisfied), and pricing is locked at placement via a draft invoice or price reservation (operations satisfied). Stakeholders must convene to agree on the window duration and draft-invoice semantics."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The requirements as stated cannot be implemented — they form a logical contradiction with zero cancellation window.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 (cancellable before shipment) is mutually exclusive with BR-2 + BR-3 combined (invoiced at placement, invoiced orders not cancellable)."}]}
